law: This feature is available in iOS 18.2 and visionOS 2.2 and later, and is available to use with calling apps that capture microphone input using Apple’s voice processing like Phone, FaceTime, and most VoIP apps.
The sample requires running on an iOS device with iOS 18.2 or later.
Before an app can add its audio to calls, a person must turn on a system-level service in the Settings app by choosing Accessibility > Audio & Visual > Add Audio in Calls.
Turning on Add Audio in Calls makes the feature available to apps on the system, but apps must explicitly request and be granted permission to use the feature.
A value of indicates the app hasn’t yet requested permission and needs to before it can use the feature.
If a person denies the app permission, the system sets the microphone injection permission state to , and the app is unable to use the feature.
If you attempt to turn on the feature in the app while in this state, the app presents an alert similar to the one shown in the previous section to update the app’s permission.
To turn on the feature, the app passes the method a value of ; to turn off the feature, it passes a value of .
The method uses the word preferred to indicate that an app can set its preference, but a person ultimately determines whether they allow the feature’s use.

To determine whether a call can use this feature, the sample awaits notification of changes to the state of the audio session’s microphone injection capabilities: When a call begins or ends, the system posts a notification of the change.
This sample code project shows you how to use an audio source node and a custom render callback to generate classic waveforms.
A class called , written in C++ to ensure real-time safety, provides the digital signal processing (DSP) logic.
To ensure glitch-free performance, audio processing must occur in a real-time safe context.
Don’t allocate memory, perform file I/O, take locks, or interact with the Swift or Objective-C runtimes when rendering audio.
Although not efficient, this approach ensures the waveforms are band-limited, and produces the maximum number of harmonics with frequencies bound by the Nyquist frequency (half the sample rate).
Synchronizing multiple audio loops with different tempos requires precise timing control — small errors in playback rate or scheduling compound over time and pull clips out of sync.
The resync timer checks every 50 ms whether any playing clips are approaching their loop boundaries.

When a clip is within 100 ms of the loop boundary, the engine reschedules the next iteration to start exactly when the current one ends: This approach maintains sample-accurate synchronization across all tracks and prevents the gradual timing drift that occurs with the built-in looping option.
The system represents the individual device microphones, and beamformers that use multiple microphones, as data sources.
In both cases, don’t change the orientation during recording.
You must run the sample on a supported physical device running iOS 14, or later.
Recording stereo audio requires the app’s audio session to use either the or category.
A speech synthesis provider allows you to bring your custom voices to iOS and macOS for system use with text-to-speech features like VoiceOver.
When the sample changes the list of available voices, it informs the system that they’re available for use by calling .
Use your custom voices to synthesize speech by building a speech synthesis provider.
See for details on how you can use this Audio Unit extension in a host app.
A class called provides the plug-in’s digital signal processing (DSP) logic, and is written in C++ to ensure real-time safety.
To ensure glitch-free performance, your plug-in’s audio processing must occur in a real-time safe context.
Don’t allocate memory, perform file I/O, take locks, or interact with the Swift or Objective-C runtimes when rendering audio.
You use a shared view controller called to coordinate the communication between the user interface and the Audio Unit.
Running an in-process plug-in requires an agreement between the host and the Audio Unit.
However, the extension target still needs to contain at least one source file for the extension binary to be created, properly loaded, and linked with the framework bundle.
To ensure the extension is created, add some unused placeholder code in your extension target, like that found in .
See for a host app you can use to load your plug-in both in-process and out-of-process.
You commonly use to add advanced real-time audio playback features to your app.
You can also use to perform offline audio processing by enabling the engine’s offline manual rendering mode.
You use offline manual rendering mode to perform advanced postprocessing tasks, such as applying effects or performing audio analysis, usually much faster than you can do in real time.
To change this default behavior, you need to explicitly configure the engine to use manual rendering mode.
The sample enables this mode by calling the method, passing it the offline rendering mode value, the audio format, and the maximum number of frames to render in a given pass of the render thread.
To use AirPlay 2 for playback to compatible output devices, configure your audio session with the route-sharing policy.
Alternatively, use a temporary queue of items, a single item, or a continuous stream of audio that has no distinct identity as a separate item.
This sample app demonstrates the use of a playlist, each item representing a single music track.
The player has public APIs that app code can use to manipulate the contents and order of the playlist, and start and stop playback.
Internally, your player should contain logic to enqueue buffers in advance of their scheduled playback time, and to handle transitions between items.
objects use objects to provide the basic audio data.
Use a to manage a playlist through a private structure: The member represents the current state of the player.
For example, you use an method to insert an item into the queue: All methods of that modify the must do so in a thread-safe manner.
Such methods issue a semaphore on entry, then use a statement to guarantee a matching semaphore on exit.
The class also contains helper methods that convert an to a , which is the required type for data passed to the audio renderer.
Before you run the sample code project in Xcode, ensure you’re using iOS 17 or later.
